summary: I am originally from Northern Somalia, where I spent my early childhood before moving away at the age of seven. Although I left when I was young, Northern Somalia is my homeland and holds a special place in my heart. When I returned in 2014, I was surprised to see how stable and developed it had become compared to what I had heard growing up.

When I was seven years old, my family and I moved to Nairobi, Kenya. We lived there for several years before my brother in America sponsored us in 2006. Nairobi was the place where I spent my formative years before coming to the United States.

Kenya was my home after leaving Northern Somalia and before moving to America. Life in Kenya was very different from the United States, especially in how people follow rules. For example, in Kenya, people often do what they want without strict adherence to rules, which was a big contrast to my experience in America.

In June 2006, my brother sponsored my family to come to the United States, and we arrived first in Chicago. The United States was a completely new world for me, with rules and order that were very different from Kenya. After Chicago, we settled in Minneapolis, Minnesota, where I started high school and eventually graduated in 2010. America felt like the land of opportunities, and I was amazed by the hospitality and lifestyle here.

Our first night in America was spent in Chicago, where we stayed in a fancy hotel that I had never seen before. It was a remarkable experience because everything was so different from Kenya, and the way people welcomed us was very warm. Chicago was our initial introduction to the United States before we moved on to Minneapolis.

After Chicago, we arrived in Minneapolis, Minnesota, where my brother had rented a house for us. It had been a long time since I had seen him, and I was excited to reunite with him. Minneapolis became our new home, and I started attending Edison High School there. My brother showed me places like all-you-can-eat buffets, which were new and amazing experiences for me.

Minnesota, especially Minneapolis, became my home after we moved to the United States in 2006. I lived there with my brother and attended Edison High School, graduating in 2010. Minnesota was where I experienced American culture firsthand and felt the opportunities this new land had to offer.

I started attending Edison High School in Minneapolis the same year we arrived in Minnesota, and I graduated in 2010. Going to Edison High was a significant part of my life as it helped me adjust to life in America and pursue my education. It marked the beginning of my new chapter in the United States.

Somalia is my homeland, and although I left when I was young, I returned in February 2014 for the first time since moving away. I was surprised to see how peaceful and developed it had become, with tall buildings and clean streets, which was very different from what I had imagined. Somalia is recovering from its difficult past, and I want people to know it is a great country with wonderful hospitality and diversity.

Growing up, I heard a lot about the Somali Civil War and how dangerous Somalia was because of it. This influenced my perception of the country as a place full of conflict and instability. However, when I visited Somalia in 2014, I saw that it was very different from those stories; the country was peaceful and recovering, which gave me hope and changed my understanding completely.

During my visit to Somalia in 2014, I noticed there were people from many parts of the world, including Europe. This diversity surprised me because I had not expected Somalia to be such a cosmopolitan place. It showed me that Somalia is more connected globally than I had thought.

When I went back to Somalia in 2014, I saw people from the Middle East living and visiting there alongside others from Europe, America, and Africa. This mix of cultures was unexpected and made me realize how diverse and welcoming Somalia really is. It challenged my previous assumptions about the country.
